subtopics.title: What's in the Box
subtopics.content:
#### Player-Count Audit
- **Supported counts** — 1 to 4 players.
- **Scaling** — Monster count and hit points scale based on scenario level and player count.
- **Solo mode** — Fully supported; control 1 character with adjusted difficulty.

#### Characters (4 Total)
- **Voidwarden** — Support class with mind-control and ally-buffing abilities.
- **Demolitionist** — Melee damage dealer specializing in obstacle destruction and area attacks.
- **Hatchet** — Ranged attacker with a signature throwing axe that can be recovered.
- **Red Guard** — Tank/defender with shield abilities and fire-based attacks.

#### Character Components (Per Character)
- **Character ability cards** — Unique hand of ability cards (varies by character: 9-11 starting cards).
- **Character board** — Tracks HP, XP, and card hand.
- **Attack modifier deck** — 20-card personal modifier deck (improved via perks).
- **Miniature** — 1 plastic miniature per character.
- **Character sheet** — Tracks level, XP, gold, perks, and items.

#### Scenario Components
- **Scenario book** — Spiral-bound book with map layouts printed directly on pages (no separate map tiles).
- **Monster standees** — Cardboard standees for all enemy types.
- **Monster stat cards** — Stat sleeves with adjustable difficulty levels.
- **Monster ability cards** — AI decks controlling monster behavior each round.
- **Overlay tiles** — Obstacles, traps, hazardous terrain, treasure tokens.
- **Condition tokens** — Poison, wound, immobilize, stun, muddle, strengthen, invisible tokens.
- **Damage tokens** — For tracking monster and summon HP.

#### Campaign Components
- **25 scenarios** — Progressive campaign with branching paths.
- **City event cards** — Narrative events between scenarios.
- **Item cards** — Equipment purchased between scenarios.
- **Tutorial scenarios** — First 5 scenarios teach mechanics gradually.

subtopics.title: Board Layout
subtopics.content:
#### Scenario Setup
1. **Open scenario book** — Turn to the indicated scenario page; the map is printed in the book.
2. **Place overlay tiles** — Add obstacles, traps, and terrain tokens as shown in the scenario description.
3. **Place monsters** — Place monster standees on hexes indicated by the scenario.
4. **Adjust difficulty** — Set monster stat sleeves to the appropriate scenario level.
5. **Shuffle monster AI decks** — Prepare ability card decks for each monster type in the scenario.
6. **Shuffle modifier decks** — Shuffle each player's attack modifier deck and the monster modifier deck.
7. **Place character miniatures** — Players place miniatures on the starting hexes.

#### Difficulty Scaling
- **Scenario level** — Average party level divided by 2 (rounded up), adjustable up or down.
- **Monster HP/attack** — Increases with scenario level.
- **Monster count** — More monsters appear at higher player counts (indicated per hex in scenario).

subtopics.title: Player Setup
subtopics.content:
#### Character Preparation
- **Select cards** — Choose your hand of ability cards for the scenario (hand size varies by character).
- **Equip items** — Slot purchased items into your equipment slots.
- **Set HP dial** — Set your HP to maximum for your current level.
- **Prepare modifier deck** — Include any perk modifications earned from leveling up.
- **Battle goal** — Draw 2 battle goal cards; choose 1 secretly (bonus XP if completed).

--- 1 Player ---
- **Solo adjustment** — Control 1 character; scenario level is your character level divided by 2 (rounded up).

--- 2-4 Players ---
- **Party composition** — Each player controls 1 character; all 4 characters are available.

subtopics.title: Starting the Game
subtopics.content:
#### Campaign Start
- **Tutorial missions** — The first 5 scenarios introduce mechanics one at a time.
- **Scenario 1** — Teaches basic movement and attack.
- **Scenario 2** — Introduces conditions and more complex abilities.
- **Scenarios 3-5** — Continue layering in remaining rules.
- **Full rules by scenario 6** — All mechanics are in play from scenario 6 onward.

#### Each Scenario Start
- **Read introduction** — Read the scenario narrative text.
- **Place components** — Set up the scenario as described.
- **Select cards** — Each player selects their hand.
- **Begin round 1** — Start with the card selection phase.

subtopics.title: Turn Structure
subtopics.content:
#### Round Structure
1. **Card selection** — Each player secretly selects 2 ability cards from their hand.
2. **Determine initiative** — The leading card's initiative number determines turn order (lower goes first).
3. **Monster initiative** — Flip the top card of each monster type's AI deck for their initiative.
4. **Resolve turns** — All figures (players and monsters) take turns in initiative order.
5. **End of round** — Check for end-of-round effects, refresh certain abilities.

#### Player Turn
- **Play 2 cards** — Use the top half of one card and the bottom half of the other.
- **Top actions** — Typically attacks and special abilities.
- **Bottom actions** — Typically movement and utility effects.
- **Default actions** — Any top can be used as Attack 2; any bottom can be used as Move 2.

#### Monster Turn
- **AI controlled** — Monsters follow their flipped ability card instructions.
- **Focus rules** — Monsters target the closest character they can attack, with specific focus tiebreakers.
- **Movement** — Monsters move the minimum distance to get in range of their focus target.

subtopics.title: Actions & Options
subtopics.content:
#### Attack Actions
- **Base attack** — Deal damage equal to the attack value.
- **Attack modifier** — Draw a card from your modifier deck to adjust damage (miss, -2, -1, +0, +1, +2, 2x, etc.).
- **Range** — Melee attacks target adjacent hexes; ranged attacks have a range value.
- **Area of effect** — Some attacks hit multiple hexes as shown on the card.

#### Movement Actions
- **Move** — Move up to the listed number of hexes.
- **Jump** — Move through occupied hexes and obstacles.
- **Flying** — Ignore all terrain and figures while moving.

#### Card Loss
- **Loss cards** — Cards marked with a loss icon (X) are removed from the game for the rest of the scenario when used.
- **Powerful effects** — Loss cards have stronger effects to compensate for being one-time use.
- **Damage prevention** — Lose 1 card from hand or 2 from discard to negate all damage from one source.

#### Rest Actions
- **Short rest** — At end of round, shuffle your discard pile and lose 1 random card to recover the rest.
- **Long rest** — Use your entire turn to recover discards (lose 1 card of your choice), heal 2 HP, and refresh spent items.

#### Conditions
- **Poison** — Target gains +1 to all damage received.
- **Wound** — Target takes 1 damage at start of each turn.
- **Stun** — Target loses their next turn.
- **Immobilize** — Target cannot move.
- **Muddle** — Target draws 2 modifier cards and uses the worse one.
- **Strengthen** — Target draws 2 modifier cards and uses the better one.
- **Invisible** — Target cannot be focused by enemies.

subtopics.title: Special Mechanics
subtopics.content:
#### Card Attrition System
- **Hand management** — Your hand of cards is your timer; as cards are lost, your options shrink.
- **Exhaustion** — When you cannot play 2 cards at the start of a round (hand + discard too small), you are exhausted and out for the scenario.
- **Strategic loss** — Choosing when to play loss cards is the core strategic tension.

#### Experience and Leveling
- **XP during scenarios** — Gain XP from specific card abilities and completing battle goals.
- **Level up** — Between scenarios, spend XP to level up, unlocking new ability cards.
- **Perks** — Leveling and completing battle goals grant perk checkmarks; perks improve your modifier deck.

#### Loot and Items
- **Loot tokens** — Pick up loot tokens by moving onto their hex; gain gold.
- **Gold between scenarios** — Spend gold to buy items from the shop.
- **Item slots** — Characters have limited equipment slots (head, body, legs, two hands, small items).
- **Item usage** — Items are spent (flipped) or consumed (returned to supply) when used; recovered on long rest or end of scenario.

#### Campaign Progression
- **Scenario unlocks** — Completing scenarios unlocks new scenarios and story branches.
- **City events** — Between scenarios, draw city event cards for narrative choices with mechanical consequences.
- **Reputation** — Choices affect reputation, which can unlock shop discounts or penalties.

subtopics.title: Endgame & Scoring
subtopics.content:
#### Scenario Victory
- **Complete objective** — Each scenario has a specific objective (usually kill all monsters in the final room).
- **All players must survive** — If all players are exhausted before the objective is met, the scenario is lost.

#### Scenario Defeat
- **Total exhaustion** — All players exhausted before objective completion.
- **Retry** — Failed scenarios can be replayed with no penalty.

#### Campaign Completion
- **25 scenarios** — The campaign concludes after completing the final scenario.
- **No competitive scoring** — This is a fully cooperative campaign with a narrative conclusion.
- **Character progression** — Characters retain all levels, items, and perks earned throughout the campaign.

#### Post-Scenario Rewards
- **Gold** — Loot tokens collected convert to gold.
- **XP** — Scenario XP and battle goal XP are tallied.
- **Bonus rewards** — Some scenarios grant bonus items, unlocks, or story rewards.

subtopics.title: Opening Priorities
subtopics.content:
- **Conserve loss cards** — Do not play loss cards in the first few rounds unless absolutely necessary; they are your clock.
- **Position matters** — Use the Demolitionist and Red Guard to control enemy positioning while Hatchet and Voidwarden operate from range.
- **Focus fire** — Kill one enemy at a time rather than spreading damage; dead enemies deal no damage.
- **Plan initiative** — Going early is important when you need to kill before enemies act; going late helps when you want to see where enemies move.
- **Loot efficiently** — Do not waste movement to pick up loot in dangerous situations.

subtopics.title: Common Mistakes
subtopics.content:
- **Burning loss cards too early** — Playing powerful loss cards in rounds 1-2 drastically shortens your stamina.
- **Ignoring rest timing** — Waiting too long to rest can leave you with no good cards.
- **Forgetting damage prevention** — You can lose cards from hand or discard to negate damage; this saves HP at the cost of stamina.
- **Poor focus understanding** — Monsters follow strict focus rules; learn them to predict enemy behavior.
- **Neglecting battle goals** — Battle goals grant perk checkmarks that permanently improve your modifier deck.

subtopics.title: Key Decisions
subtopics.content:
#### Decision 1: Stamina vs Power
- **Conserve cards** — Play non-loss cards to stay in the fight longer.
- **Use loss cards** — Play powerful loss cards to solve immediate crises.

#### Decision 2: Initiative Manipulation
- **Go early** — Select a card with low initiative to act before enemies.
- **Go late** — Select high initiative to see enemy movements before committing.

#### Decision 3: Short Rest vs Long Rest
- **Short rest** — Faster but you lose a random card from discard.
- **Long rest** — Costs your entire turn but lets you choose which card to lose and heals 2 HP.
